questionText: What is the maximum number of service disconnects allowed for a single service?
explanation: NEC 230.71(A) limits the number of service disconnecting means to a maximum of 6 switches or circuit breakers, mounted in a single enclosure or grouped together.
sparkyTip: Six throws max to turn off the service - remember the 'rule of six!'

questionText: What is the minimum service size required for a single-family dwelling?
explanation: NEC 230.79(C) requires a minimum 100-ampere, 3-wire service for a single-family dwelling with an initial installation of more than two 2-wire branch circuits.
sparkyTip: 100 amps minimum for homes - the modern standard for residential service!

questionText: Service entrance conductors must be sized to carry at least what percentage of the calculated load?
explanation: NEC 230.42(A)(1) requires service entrance conductors to have an ampacity not less than 100% of the maximum load to be served after applying demand factors.
sparkyTip: Service conductors at 100% of calculated load - after demand factors are applied!

questionText: Where must the service disconnecting means be located?
options: At a readily accessible location nearest the point of entrance
explanation: NEC 230.70(A)(1) requires the service disconnecting means to be installed at a readily accessible location either outside the building or inside nearest the point of entrance.
sparkyTip: Service disconnect near the entry point - firefighters need quick access!

questionText: What is the purpose of a drip loop on service entrance conductors?
options: To prevent water from entering the raceway
explanation: NEC 230.54(F) requires service entrance conductors to have a drip loop to prevent water from entering the service raceway or equipment. Water runs down and drips off the loop.
sparkyTip: Drip loops keep water out - always form that loop below the weatherhead!
